item.title: Cold-Climate Gardening in USDA Zone 5b
item.description:
Cold-Climate Gardening in USDA Zone 5b

Cold-climate gardening in USDA Zone 5b is not a scaled-down version of temperate agriculture. It is a distinct practice shaped by winter temperature extremes, compressed growing seasons, and environmental parameters that turn apparent constraints into structural advantages for specific crops and cultivation strategies.

Zone 5b encompasses regions where minimum average winter temperatures range from -15°F to -10°F. In the Lake Superior region—exemplified by Ironwood, Michigan, in the western Upper Peninsula—this means last spring frosts arrive around May 25 and first fall frosts by September 20, yielding 110–130 frost-free days. This timeline appears restrictive only when measured against warm-climate baselines. Zone 5b gardening instead establishes its own coherent system of plant-place fit, seasonal timing, and soil work.

item.title: Guild Planting and Companion Planting
item.description:
What Is a Plant Guild?

A plant guild is not a haphazard collection but an ecological design: a grouped set of plants that interact across multiple dimensions—nutrient cycling, pest suppression, pollinator attraction, structural support—to create a more stable and productive system than any single plant could sustain alone. The term bridges horticultural practice and ecological theory, treating a garden plot not as monoculture production space but as a designed ecosystem with functional roles and interdependencies.

item.title: How Soil Feeds Plants
item.description:
How Soil Feeds Plants

The Practical Question: Why Does Compost Work?

A gardener amends their bed with compost, waits a season, and plants grow noticeably better. The soil holds water longer. Roots develop without added fertilizer. Pests seem less destructive. This is not magic. It is the consequence of how soil actually works — and understanding it transforms composting from a folk practice into a literacy in plant ecology.

What Soil Actually Is

Soil is not dirt. Dirt is the absence of soil — sterile, inert mineral particles. Soil is a living composite.

item.title: Korean Natural Farming
item.description:
Korean Natural Farming (KNF) is not merely an organic technique but a systematic approach to soil ecology grounded in the hypothesis that plants thrive when cultivated within their native microbial context. Developed by Cho Han Kyu beginning in the 1960s and systematized by the 1980s, KNF treats soil not as inert substrate but as a living community whose reconstruction—not replacement—generates fertility and resilience.

The Core Principle: Matching Microbial Context to Place

Conventional agriculture disrupts soil structure through synthetic fungicides, fumigants, and intensive tillage. These disrupt the microbial populations that have co-evolved with local plant species and soil chemistry. Healthy soil contains billions of organisms per gram: bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and nematodes whose metabolic activities make nutrients bioavailable to plants.

item.title: Lunar Gardening Traditions
item.description:
Lunar Gardening Traditions

Lunar gardening—the practice of timing planting, tending, and harvesting by the moon’s phases—appears across virtually every agricultural civilization. Anishinaabe, Māori, Chinese, Vedic Indian, Mesoamerican, European, and African farming traditions all developed similar protocols for moon-based cultivation. This convergence across independent agricultural societies suggests not superstition but empirical observation codified into working calendars. The evidence base is contested, but the consensus among practitioners across five continents is stable enough to warrant serious analysis of what they actually claim and what we can assess about the underlying mechanisms.

item.title: Reading a Seed Packet
item.description:
Reading a Seed Packet

The Seed Packet as Information Artifact

A seed packet is not a casual wrapper. It is a compressed growing guide, every specification reflecting actual plant biology. Reading it correctly transforms guesswork into precision.

The packet must communicate across climates, across skill levels, and across decades — a seed lot from 2019 must still be usable in 2026. The information is dense because it must be. It is also cryptic to many gardeners because the terminology reflects scientific plant biology, not casual gardening parlance. This gap is the focus here.

item.title: Season Extension for Short-Season Climates
item.description: A short growing season is fundamentally a thermal deficit: the number of frost-free days, accumulated heat (measured in growing degree days), and soil temperature at critical growth stages do not match the biological requirements of plants a gardener wants to grow. In a 110–130 day season, many valuable crops — tomatoes, peppers, eggplant, southern peas — become marginal or impossible. Season extension techniques are thermal engineering solutions that manipulate the energy budget of soil and air to artificially extend the period when these plants can grow.

item.title: Seed Saving as Cultural Practice
item.description: Seed saving is not gardening — it is the deliberate transmission of genetic material as cultural memory, embedded in family relationships, ceremonial practice, and resistance to the homogenization of food. When a farmer saves seeds from this year’s harvest to plant next season, she reproduces not just a plant but a lineage of knowledge: how to recognize maturity, when to harvest for seed rather than consumption, which plants to select as parents for the next generation. Over decades and generations, this selective pressure shapes varieties toward the conditions and preferences of a specific place and people. The seed becomes an archive of human intention, a biological record of what mattered enough to preserve.

item.title: Three Sisters Polyculture
item.description:
Three Sisters Polyculture

The Gift Framework

In Haudenosaunee creation narratives, the daughter of Skywoman died in childbirth, and from her body emerged three sustaining foods: corn from her breasts (O:nenhste), beans from her hands (ohsaheta), and squash from her bellybutton (onon’onhsera). In the Mohawk language, the system is Kionhekwa — “They give us life.”

This is not metaphor. The three plants form a mutually dependent system that has sustained communities across the Great Lakes region and throughout the Americas for over two thousand years. The system works because each plant solves a problem the others create, and together they solve problems neither could address alone.

item.title: Why Plants Bolt
item.description:
Why Plants Bolt

The Frustration: Success Looks Like Failure

A gardener has been harvesting tender lettuce leaves for three weeks. Then, overnight, a flower stalk shoots through the center. The leaves turn bitter. The plant has become inedible. The gardener regards this as failure — the plant bolted, the crop is lost.

From the plant’s perspective, this is success. The plant accomplished its reproductive goal. The gardener’s timeline (harvest leaves) and the plant’s timeline (produce seeds) have collided.
